key_insight: Welding residual stresses in gusset joints are more meaningfully interpreted by splitting the total stress into primary, secondary, and tertiary components, because the components vary much more with geometry and boundary conditions than the total residual stress does.
engineering_relevance: For welded structural design, the study shows that support condition, support release, and joint geometry can strongly affect local stress distributions and therefore the assessed load-carrying capacity.
method: Fully coupled thermo-mechanical finite element welding simulation with a validated heat source.
Computed residual stress near a longitudinally welded-around gusset made from quenched-and-tempered 690 steel.
Compared total toe stress with decomposed membrane, bending, and nonlinear tertiary components across plate width and thickness under multiple boundary conditions.
evidence_signal: The parametric study varied three plate widths, five support configurations, and two support distances, showing limited change in total residual stress but large changes in the individual stress components.
watch_item: The conclusions depend on simulation assumptions, boundary-condition choices, and the specific gusset geometry/material, so direct transfer to other welded joints should be done cautiously.
paper_title: Classification of welding-induced residual stresses in gusset joints
